consensus_findings.description: Inconsistency between design.md and graph-client/spec.md on what GraphClient.paginate() yields. design.md D4 states 'yields each page's value array as a whole list, not individual records'. graph-client/spec.md scenario 'Paginate yields successive pages until nextLink absent' states 'Each yielded item SHALL be the full page response (a dict with value, @odata.nextLink, and other top-level keys)'. The two contradict each other and cannot both be implemented.

graph-client/spec.md does not require honoring HTTP 429 Retry-After headers. Microsoft Graph uses 429 with Retry-After heavily for throttling, and the standard exponential backoff in P9's DEFAULT_HTTP_RETRY_POLICY ignores Retry-After entirely. Without explicit Retry-After honoring, GraphClient will retry too soon during throttling, increasing the chance of breaker trip and prolonging recovery.

graph-client/spec.md does not specify a per-request timeout for GraphClient HTTP calls. Without a timeout, a hung TCP connection could block an extension tool indefinitely. P9's @resilient_http handles retry but does not by itself enforce timeouts at the httpx layer.

The new transport layer (core/graph_client.py) makes outbound HTTP calls but has no transport-level observability requirement. Existing observability spans (trace_tool_call, trace_llm_call) cover the extension and harness tiers but not the Graph HTTP tier. Debugging auth refresh, rate limiting, and 401 patterns will be harder than necessary.

design.md D10 says MSAF harness skips MemoryPolicy in P5, deferred to P5b. This creates user-visible asymmetry: DeepAgents harness on the personal persona has memory continuity, MSAF harness on the work persona does not. The work persona is precisely the one most likely to benefit from cross-session memory (recurring reports, ongoing email triage, etc). Treating this as 'documented follow-up' understates the UX impact.

graph-client/spec.md scenario 'Page ceiling triggers termination with warning' silently truncates pagination after 100 pages with only a log warning. Callers (and the agent invoking the extension tool) have no programmatic way to detect that truncation occurred. A user could see a partial result and assume completeness.

The MSAL token cache lives at personas/<name>/.cache/msal_token_cache.json, inside the private persona submodule mount point. Task 7.3 amends personas/_template/.gitignore but this only helps NEW personas. An EXISTING persona submodule that was created before this template change does not pick up the new ignore rule, and accidental commits of the cache file would leak refresh tokens to a private (or worse, public) repository.

ms-extensions/spec.md uses the phrase 'Default scopes include X, Y, Z' for each extension. The spec does not define what happens when persona.yaml provides scopes — does the persona override REPLACE, MERGE-INTO, or ADD-TO the defaults? Implementation will pick one but spec is silent, which means future implementations might disagree.

When the per-extension circuit breaker is OPEN and an extension tool is invoked, the spec does not specify what error the tool raises. P9 default behavior is BreakerOpen exception, but ms-extensions/spec.md should be explicit so callers (agents) get a predictable error class.

tasks.md task 7.7 piggybacks the P4 observability roadmap-drift hygiene fix into wp-integration. While the fix is one line, bundling it with P5 means the hygiene improvement is blocked if P5 review fails. Splitting it into a separate small commit (or a separate PR) would let the hygiene fix land independently.

ms-agent-framework-harness/spec.md scenario 'Authorized extensions are filtered through ToolPolicy' requires the harness to consult ToolPolicy.authorized_extensions before reading as_ms_agent_tools. But the existing capability-resolver spec says the resolver does this filtering, not the harness. There may be a layering question: does the resolver pass already-filtered extensions to create_agent, or does the harness re-filter? If both filter, scenarios may double-count exclusions.

If a work persona uses an Entra app registration that lacks one of the spec's default scopes (e.g., persona has Mail.Read but not Mail.Send), the extension tool calls will get HTTP 403 at first invocation — late binding error. The plan does not specify any startup-time scope verification.

The plan requires real MS extensions to accept an injected GraphClient, and scenarios call create_extension(config={}, client=mock_client), but the existing PersonaRegistry.load_extensions path still calls mod.create_extension(config) and no spec/task updates the registry or capability resolver to create an MSALStrategy and per-extension GraphClient. This leaves the real extensions unconstructable through the production extension-loading path.

CloudGraphClient only supports JSON dict GET/POST responses, but ms-extensions declares sharepoint.download_document. A real SharePoint/Drive content download needs bytes, streaming, or a redirect/content endpoint contract; it cannot be represented safely by get() -> dict[str, Any].

GraphClient POST is specified to return a parsed dict for every 2xx response, but outlook.send_email posts to /me/sendMail, which commonly returns a successful empty 202 response. A mandatory JSON parse will fail on the planned write tool's success path.

D4 and the graph-client spec say every GraphClient HTTP call is wrapped with P9 resilient_http, but GraphAPIError is the required exception for non-2xx responses. Existing resilient_http retries httpx.HTTPStatusError/status-coded httpx failures, not an arbitrary custom GraphAPIError, so 429/5xx Graph responses may never retry or trip the breaker as intended.

The plan applies the same retry layer to all outbound Graph calls, including non-idempotent POST writes send_email and post_chat_message. Retrying those after a timeout, 429, or 5xx can send duplicate emails or chat messages because no idempotency key, retry classification, or write-specific policy is specified.
The specs mention 429 through P9 resilience but do not require Microsoft Graph Retry-After handling. Default exponential backoff can violate Graph throttling guidance and amplify rate-limit failures.

MSAL's Python token acquisition APIs are synchronous, while MSALStrategy exposes async acquire_token. The specs do not require blocking MSAL calls, including interactive/device-code/client-credentials flows, to be run off the event loop.

Token Cache File Discipline requires the final cache file to be 0o600, but does not require the temporary msal_token_cache.json.tmp file to be created with restrictive permissions before secret material is written. On a permissive umask, the temp file can be briefly group/world readable.

Tool inputs such as message_id, chat_id, site/document identifiers, and OData search strings are interpolated into Graph paths/params, but ms-extensions does not specify path-segment escaping, allowlisting, or input validation. Malformed IDs could alter paths or query semantics before reaching GraphClient.

Observability requirements preserve trace_tool_call and trace_llm_call, but there is no normative transport-level Graph span/log requirement for method, sanitized path, status_code, request_id/client-request-id, retry count, or throttling. Resilience spans alone do not identify which Graph operation failed.

Strategy Selection by Persona Configuration references persona.auth.ms.flow and D8 shows auth.ms YAML, but the current PersonaConfig exposes auth_provider/auth_config plus raw, and core/persona.py currently resolves auth.config rather than an auth.ms subtree. The plan does not specify whether PersonaConfig changes, raw access, or a parser migration owns this schema.

GraphClient lacks an explicit requirement to handle HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) by parsing and respecting the 'Retry-After' header. While P9 @resilient_http provides generic retry/backoff, Microsoft Graph best practices mandate following Retry-After to avoid escalating service-level blocks.

GraphClient does not specify a request timeout configuration. The default httpx timeout (5s) is likely insufficient for heavy Microsoft Graph operations such as searching large mailboxes (Outlook) or deep profile searches (People), while an unbounded timeout poses a risk of hanging the agent session.

The GraphClient spec does not require capturing the 'request-id' response header on successful calls. These IDs are the primary key for correlating client-side telemetry with Microsoft's internal Entra ID and Graph API audit logs during troubleshooting.

The contract for 'sharepoint.download_document' return value is unspecified. Since tool outputs are consumed by LLMs, returning binary data or a raw file stream will cause serialization failures in both LangChain and MSAF harnesses.
